question: How to market garden?
title: How to Market Garden: A Real-World Guide to Turning Your Garden Into a Business
metaDescription: Market gardening is selling what you grow. Scotty shares how he took his Houston backyard from hobby garden to farmers market booth — what works, what doesn't, and how to start.
content:
I want to tell you something upfront: I had a friend who said I live on pipe dreams. He meant it kindly enough, but the message was clear — the idea of growing enough food in my backyard in the middle of Houston, Texas to go sell at a farmers market was, in his estimation, a stretch.

He's not entirely wrong that it's a stretch. But I'm doing it anyway. And I'm going to tell you exactly how.

Market gardening is the practice of growing vegetables, herbs, and other produce specifically for direct sale — at farmers markets, to restaurants, through a CSA, or to neighbors and friends. It sits between home gardening and full-scale commercial farming. It doesn't require hundreds of acres. It doesn't require expensive equipment. What it requires is intention, planning, and a willingness to match what you grow with what people in your community actually want to buy.

Here's how to get started.

---

## Start Small and Know Your Land

The single biggest mistake new market gardeners make is scaling too fast. You don't need a lot of land to test whether market gardening works for you. You need a small, well-managed piece of land and a clear picture of what it can realistically produce.

Bootstrap Farmer's market gardening guide recommends starting with whatever land you have access to — even a fraction of an acre — before expanding. The economics of market gardening hinge on getting good production from limited space, so learning intensive growing methods on a small plot first gives you real data before you commit to more land, more infrastructure, and more investment.

For me, it started with my backyard in Houston. A pretty good size for a city lot, but still a city lot. I wasn't going to grow corn or wheat — that's what large farms do at a scale that makes it impossible to compete. What I was going to grow was things that have high value per square foot, sell well at market, and are difficult to find locally: microgreens, specialty herbs, baby salad mix, fermented vegetables, hot peppers, heirloom tomatoes.

Know your soil before you grow in it. Living soil — biologically active, aggregated, rich in organic matter — is what separates a productive market garden from a struggle. If your soil is depleted, the first investment you make should be compost. That is not optional. You cannot build a productive market garden on dead dirt.

## Plan Your Crops Around the Market

This is where a lot of people get it backwards. They grow what they like, then they try to sell it. A market gardener has to flip that. You grow what sells.

Spend time at your local farmers markets before you set up a booth. What are people buying? What are vendors selling out of versus hauling back home? Where are the gaps? The ATTRA sustainable agriculture market gardening guide is clear on this: you need a marketing plan before you put seeds in the ground. Not after. Before.

Leafy greens — arugula, kale, spinach, swiss chard, mizuna — sell consistently at farmers markets across the season. They're fast-growing, they can be succession planted (meaning you stagger plantings every two to three weeks so you always have something ready), and they provide reliable revenue. Herbs do well. Specialty items — fermented vegetables, edible flowers, uncommon varieties — create points of difference that get people to your booth.

Avoid crops where you're competing with industrial scale. Corn, potatoes, and basic tomatoes are commodities at the scale commercial farms produce them. Go where your biology and your attention can produce something a large farm can't: diversity, freshness, story, and a face that goes with the food.

## Know Your Sales Channels

Farmers markets are the obvious entry point. The foot traffic, the direct customer relationships, the cash flow on market day — all of it makes sense for a starting market gardener. But there are a few things to understand going in.

Your first few markets are going to be learning experiences. My third market was a big milestone for me — I was trying to break $500 in a single day, which felt like a big deal at that scale. I set up my booth, priced my products, and learned more from those three hours of conversation with customers than I had from months of planning. You learn what people respond to, what questions they ask, what makes them buy.

Beyond farmers markets, consider Community Supported Agriculture — CSA subscriptions where customers pay upfront at the start of the season and receive weekly boxes of produce. It's a cash flow game-changer. That upfront payment helps you buy seeds, supplies, and inputs before the season starts rather than spending down savings and hoping market sales cover you.

Restaurants are another channel worth cultivating. Find chefs who build seasonal menus and care about where their food comes from. The ATTRA guide points out that chefs who advertise their local sourcing are natural partners — they send customers your way, you supply them with fresh, differentiated product. It's a relationship business, not just a transaction.

## The Tools and Infrastructure You Actually Need

Here's the good news: you don't need much to get started. Good soil, good seeds, basic hand tools, and a booth setup for market day. That's it for the first season.

As you scale, you'll add things — more growing space, drip irrigation to reduce labor, a small hoophouse or caterpillar tunnel to extend your season and protect crops in heavy rain, a good cooler for harvest and transport. But none of that has to come day one.

What you do need from day one is record-keeping. Track what you plant, when you plant it, how it produces, what it sells for, and what doesn't sell. That data is what lets you make better decisions next season. Market gardening is iterative. Every season teaches you something the previous one couldn't.

A good display matters too. At market, presentation is part of the product. Clean, organized, visually appealing. Signage that explains what you grow and how you grow it. People at a farmers market are there because they want connection to their food — give them that.

## Soil First: The Foundation of Everything

I want to dwell on this before we get to the economics, because most market gardening guides skip it or treat it as an afterthought. The soil is not an afterthought. The soil is the whole thing.

A productive market garden is not built on depleted, compacted, biologically dead dirt. You can force crops to grow in bad soil with enough synthetic fertilizer, but you'll spend more money, have more disease pressure, produce nutritionally inferior food, and fight an uphill battle every season. The market gardener who builds their soil biology first has a fundamentally different experience — better yield, better flavor, better resilience, lower input costs.

Biologically active soil — the kind with good aggregate structure, high organic matter, and a thriving food web — holds water better, which means you water less. It makes nutrients plant-available through biological processes, which means you fertilize less. It suppresses disease organisms through competitive exclusion, which means you spray less or not at all. Every dollar you invest in compost and cover crops is a dollar you don't spend on irrigation, fertilizer, and pesticides.

For a small market garden operating on tight margins, this matters enormously. The Permaculture Apprentice analysis of profitable small-scale market gardening consistently points to soil quality as the primary driver of profitability at the scale where labor is your biggest cost. Better soil means faster plant growth, which means faster turnover, which means more harvests per season from the same beds.

Build the soil. Start a compost pile. Add organic matter. That is not optional — it is the investment with the highest return.

## The Real Point of Market Gardening

I want to be honest about this: market gardening out of a backyard is not going to replace a full-time income in most cases. That's not the point, at least not where I started.

The point, for me, is growing food that is biologically alive — food that still carries the microbial communities of the living soil it was grown in — and connecting my neighbors in Houston to that food. These food calories are living calories. That's something the grocery store simply cannot offer.

It's also a practice. A discipline of watching the soil, tending it, understanding what it's doing and what it needs. Gabe Brown talks about the producer being a manager of the biological processes on their land — not a chemical applicator, not a machine operator, but a manager of living systems. Market gardening at the backyard scale is where a lot of people first experience what that means.

Start small. Grow intentionally. Sell what you grow. Learn what the market wants. Come back next season with better data and better soil.

That's how you market garden.

---

2. ATTRA / NCAT — Market Gardening: A Start Up Guide: https://attra.ncat.org/publication/market-gardening-a-start-up-guide/

scottyQuotes: I had a friend who said I live on pipe dreams. He meant it kindly, but I'm doing it anyway.
I'm looking to go into farmers markets and sell what I am growing out in my backyard right here in the middle of Houston, Texas.
It's my third market, and hopefully I can break 500. That's my big goal.
